behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract
behaviors.justification: The system must correctly extract specific contractual terms from real documents using LLM analysis. Correctness depends on what the real LLM produces—not on pre-programmed logic. A mocked LLM would hide the core failure: the real LLM failing to recognize or extract certain term types (e.g., missing indirect penalty references, misattributing obligations). Testing requires real document analysis with real LLM capability.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test contract with 5-10 known key terms (specific party names, effective dates, measurable obligations, explicit penalty amounts). Run the clause extractor on this real contract via the actual LLM integration. Verify extraction accuracy: all known terms are identified, correctly attributed to the right contract section, not hallucinated, and correctly categorized.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test must run at Agentic level because the core behavior—identifying specific terms from unstructured contract text—is fundamentally dependent on LLM reasoning capability. Any mock would short-circuit the real failure mode where the LLM misses or misinterprets contractual language.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic
behaviors.justification: Detecting euphemistic risk language (e.g., 'may reconsider' for automatic termination, 'favorable circumstances' for unlimited liability) is inherently a nuanced LLM reasoning task. Mocking the risk assessor with fixed flag values would verify only the logic chain, not the critical capability: recognition of subtle, indirect problem language. Real failures occur when the LLM misses euphemistic phrasing that the policy forbids.
behaviors.test_description: Create test contracts containing 3-5 known euphemistic risk clauses (indirect refusals, hidden liabilities, ambiguous termination triggers). Configure a risk policy that forbids these problem patterns. Run the risk assessor on real contracts with real LLM calls. Verify that euphemistic clauses are correctly flagged as problematic, even when not explicitly named in the policy.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires Agentic level because euphemism detection relies on non-deterministic LLM reasoning about linguistic nuance. A Unit or Integration test (mocking the assessor's output) would only verify that flagged items are processed—not that the LLM actually recognizes the subtle language patterns.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Regulatory compliance verification is jurisdiction-specific and requires the LLM to reason about complex, domain-specific rules. Mocking the compliance checker would miss real failures: the LLM applying wrong regulations, misunderstanding jurisdiction-specific constraints, or missing subtle compliance requirements (e.g., required disclosures, arbitration waivers, data handling mandates). Each jurisdiction's requirements demand real LLM reasoning.
behaviors.test_description: Select 2-3 test contracts designed to violate known regulatory requirements in a specific jurisdiction (e.g., missing GDPR data privacy disclosures, missing arbitration language for US enforceability, inadequate consumer protection language). Run the compliance checker for that jurisdiction via real LLM. Verify that all known violations are detected and correctly classified by jurisdiction.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Must run at Agentic level because regulatory reasoning is domain knowledge only a real LLM can apply. Mocking would skip the step that matters: LLM judgment about whether a contract satisfies jurisdiction-specific legal rules and constraints.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution
behaviors.justification: The core failure modes are merge logic bugs: dropping one assessment, losing attribution data, or incorrectly combining conflicting outputs. These can be caught by testing the merge step wired to mock reviewer modules with known conflicting outputs. Integration-level testing is sufficient because the test focuses on the merge algorithm's correctness, not on whether real reviewers actually disagree (that requires Workflow testing). Mock conflict scenarios directly exercise the merge's conflict-handling code path.
behaviors.test_description: Mock two reviewer outputs for the same contract clause: reviewer A marks it as 'extraction: parties ABC Corp, standard terms', and reviewer B marks it as 'risk assessment: high-risk indemnity clause, severity critical'. Wire these into the merge step. Verify the merged report: (1) includes both assessments in full, (2) clearly attributes each to its source reviewer, (3) does not drop or overwrite either assessment, (4) preserves the semantic meaning of both findings in the output.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration-level testing with mocked inputs is sufficient for the merge logic itself. However, this test doesn't verify that real reviewers actually disagree on real contracts—that's covered by the Workflow test (behavior 5). A lower level wouldn't exercise the conflict-merging code path; a higher level would add unnecessary LLM non-determinism.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline—dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report—completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires multiple agentic steps in sequence: (1) dispatch real LLM calls to three independent reviewers in parallel, (2) collect their asynchronous results, (3) merge them into a unified report. Mocking any reviewer would hide real failures: async timing issues, output format mismatches between real LLM and merge logic, partial failures in one reviewer, transient errors, or data loss during aggregation. The behavior explicitly requires testing with 'a real contract', implying real document processing.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full end-to-end pipeline on a real test contract. Dispatch it to all three real reviewers (clause extractor, risk assessor, compliance checker) in parallel, allowing each to make actual LLM calls asynchronously. Collect all three results (handling async completion). Merge them into a unified report. Verify: (1) pipeline completes without error, timeout, or resource exhaustion, (2) unified report contains findings from all three reviewers, (3) no findings are dropped, duplicated, or corrupted, (4) report structure is schema-valid and semantically complete.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires Workflow level because the test orchestrates multiple agentic steps (three parallel real LLM calls + merge). Mocking would skip real failure modes: async coordination bugs (race conditions, lost results from slow reviewers), output format mismatches with real LLM responses, partial failures where one reviewer times out, or aggregation errors that only appear under actual concurrency.
